# Team Profile

# 97265A: The Jagbots

97265A, the Glen Allen Jagbots has been around for 5 years. As a team with 23 members, we are dedicated to continuous improvement, communication, and hard work.

This engineering notebook documents the complete design, build, and programming journey of our VEX V5 robot from September through December 2025. It reflects our engineering process in its entirety, including brainstorming, failed attempts, redesigns, testing data, and final solutions. Each entry demonstrates collaboration between Build, Code, and Strategy subteams, and decisions grounded in teamwork.

Reflecting on Last Year

# Retrospect: Over Under, 2024-25

Purpose: The document is made to reflect and improve from the mistakes and successes from last year.

| WENT WELL                                                                           | NEEDS IMPROVEMENT                                                                              |
| ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| We communicated well with the team members.                                         | We didn’t have solid leadership and had lots of disagreements within the leadership team.      |
| Our final design worked well and allowed us to score rings efficiently.             | Overall efficiency, many members relied too heavily on leadership to build or generate ideas.  |
| We started consulting adults with experience in order to figure out how to improve. | We struggled with time management and making goals for finishing different parts of the robot. |
| We had lots of team building activities.                                            | We did not have formal design plans.                                                           |
| Block code was not as professional as we wanted it to be.                           | We did not do much research on the notebook and it was therefore lacking.                      |
| We didn’t have all of the parts necessary so there was a lot of improvisation.      |                                                                                                |

This year, we are committed to becoming more organized, researched, and prepared for competitions.

# Identify

# Push Back: Identify the Game Problem

# Field Elements:

- Blocks: The main scoring objects
- Goals: Two types—Long Goals and Center Goals
- Loaders: Four stations that start with six blocks each

The VEX Robotics Competition game for 2025-2026 is Push Back. High stakes is played with 2 alliances each trying to score more than the other. The main scoring elements are the blue and red balls. The goals are very high so we need some height on the robot, there is also a low goal so we need to have a versatile robot. Since there are different heights to the goals, we will need to make sure the robot can output at different heights easily. Alliances could be hard for us because we need a good teammate that specializes in a different aspect of the game to benefit ours.

# INACTIVITY

# Identify the Problem

# PROBLEM STATEMENT

Team members would consistently sit idly playing games and goofing off instead of helping build or code robot or build the field.

# GOALS

- Everyone should be active with building the robot so we can finish its final build weeks before competitions.
- Everyone should be active building the field so we can get an ample amount of time to test the robot and its code.

# CONSTRAINTS

- Some people not listening when told to do work.
- These people would also distract other people into not working.

# Selecting Designs

# REASONS FOR SELECTING THE FOUR MOTOR DRIVEBASE

- Using 6 motors for the drive base alone, as we originally planned, leaves us with only 2 motors for everything else. This causes problems so we switched to using only 4 motors.
- 4 motors gives us enough power from the base to have a larger robot without weight issues.

# Test of Speed

This test measures how fast the drivetrain goes in a straight line. This is useful as this is the regular way of movement of the robot in game, skills, and autonomous. We are also going to test how the drivetrain performs with 4 and 6 motors. We will have 2 sets of 5 trials for this test measures how fast the drivetrain moves in a straight line. This is useful because it allows for smooth movement of the robot in the game. Additionally, we are testing how the drivetrain performs with 4 and 6 motors.

| -------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Testing how fast the Robot is with 4 motors and 6 motors | Timing how fast it takes for the robot to go from one wall of the field to the other (6 tiles) | The robot should be able to finish the test in 2.5 seconds or less |

# Intake

# Brainstorm Solutions

# Elevator intake

Series of elevators moving balls through the robot, from one end to the other.

| Pros            | Cons                            |
| --------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Space efficient | Slower                          |
|                 | More complicated for less value |
|                 | Too much potential for jams     |

# Ball Bag Intake

Balls are intake using rubber band mechanism and ramp. They are then stored in a bag or bucket in the middle. The balls are then taken through a vertically moving conveyor.

| Pros               | Cons                           |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------ |
| Simple             | Complicated to score with      |
| Lots of storage    | That many balls is not optimal |
| Only uses 3 motors |                                |

# Magazine Intake

Balls are taken into 6 lines that rotate, like a revolver. The cartridges are then rotated by a motor and unloaded using a pneumatic piston.

| Pros           | Cons                      |
| -------------- | ------------------------- |
| Lot of storage | Complicated to score with |
| Innovative     | Difficult to implement    |
|                | Contrived design          |

# N-Shaped Intake

Series of 3 ramps. First ramp moves them vertically up then drops them into a second ramp. The second ramp goes downward into a third ramp. The third ramp is lined with flywheels that move balls vertically up and score them.

| Pros               | Cons                                        |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------- |
| Efficient          | Lacks middle goal scoring                   |
| Name: Space Saving | Once in the middle ramp balls can't go back |

Cons:

- * Faster on green motors (even with weight)
* Can hold more weight on green
Cons:
* More motors
* Gear drive difficult to build together
* Chain drive difficult to work on |

# Selected Base: Six Motor Gear Drive

Because of what we saw from prior competitions as well as in-meeting testing we have determined that a 6 motor drive is preferable because the increased speed and weight capacity outweighs the increased usage of motors. We also decided on a gear drive because they have been much more reliable.

# About Us

GAHS Robotics Team

Robotics is a great way to learn engineering, problem-solving, and teamwork skills. It challenges students to work in teams to design, build, and program a robot for competition against other schools. If you're looking for a club to get hands-on experience in engineering, problem-solving, and building robots, then robotics is for you!

# Our Strengths:

- Build: Engineer solutions to complex problems and build competition robots using different parts.
- Code: Create lines of code to direct the robot and control its movements. Learn new forms of code like JavaScript and block-coding.
- Strategy: Strategize with your teammates about the best course of action on how to score the most points.

# Why you should join!

- Communication & Teamwork
- Learn New Skills
- Hands-on STEM Experience
- Potential Leadership opportunities

# Fun Facts:

- In 2025, our team earned a "luxdoes" Award.
- We are hosting several events at GAHS.
- You can earn more than 20 volunteer hours helping to prepare.

# About Us

GAHS Robotics Team

Robotics is a great way to learn engineering, problem-solving, and teamwork skills. It challenges students to work in teams to design, build, and program a robot for competition against other schools. If you're looking for a club to get hands-on experience in engineering, problem-solving, and building robots, then robotics is for you!

# Our Strengths:

- Build: Engineer solutions to complex problems and build competition robots using different parts.
- Code: Create lines of code to direct the robot and control its movements. Learn new forms of code like JavaScript and block-coding.
- Strategy: Strategize with your teammates about the best course of action on how to score the most points.

# Why you should join!

- Communication & Teamwork
- Learn New Skills
- Hands-on STEM Experience
- Potential Leadership opportunities

# Fun Facts:

- In 2025, our team earned a "luxdoes" Award.
- We are hosting several events at GAHS.
- You can earn more than 20 volunteer hours helping to prepare.

# Team Meeting - 9/03

# Drivetrain Code

- We wanted to be able to control each side of the robots motors in drive mode so we couldn't use the built in drivetrain in the code and we did not have an inertial sensor so we had 2 options left:
- - Time-based drivetrain, The time for the motors to spin is precalculated and it spins for that exact time
- Pros are that its very simple
- Cons are that motor efficiency can change over time, which a precalculation cannot account for
- Rotation based drivetrain, how much the motor spins is determined by how much it has left to spin
- Pros are that it can be very accurate if you stop the robot and let it go it will still go to its desired goal
- Can make robot slow down as it approaches the goal to prevent jerking of robot
- Cons are that its very complex and easy to make mistakes in code

Complexity was not a problem for us and so we started to work on the rotation based drivetrain.

# Rotation Based Drivetrain

This system will get how much the robot needs to move by using the math of the robots dimensions.

- First we got how much the wheels must move with:
to_move = ((math.fabs(D) / wheel_circumference) * 360) / 6
- D is, in inches, how much we want to move

Then, we made a while loop that loops until to_move <= 0, because to_move says how much rotations left the wheels have left to rotate.

- We calculated the motor efficiency, velocity, and time it took to get from each frame with the equation:
delta = (((velocity * (efficiency / 100)) / 60) * (elasped_time)) * 360
- Velocity = RPM
- Was done for each side of the robot separately then we got the average delta.

We then used this to get how much the robot moved with:
to_move = to_move + (0 - (math.fabs(Ldelta) + math.fabs(Rdelta)) / 8)

- L and R delta are for each side of the robot respectively.
- 4 motors * 2 = 8, which is the divisor.

We also made the robot slow down as it approached the target velocity with:
velset = (abs_tomove * (max_speed - floor_speed) + floor_speed)

- abs_tomove = abs(to_move / starting_to_move)
- Starting_to_move is the unchanged starting to_move.

All of this was put in a while to_move > 0 so once the to_move was 0 or less, the robot would set all of the motors velocities to zero, completing the movement.

We also wanted the robot to turn with a system similar to the movement system.

- We use the calculations:
- turn_circumference = 2.0 * math.pi * (wheelbase / 2.0)
- circumference = 2.0 * math.pi * wheel_radius
- to_move = ((((math.fabs(d) / 360.0) * (turn_circumference / 2.0)) / circumference) * 360.0) / 2.0
- In replacement of the original and the velocities of each side are either positive or negative to make the robot turn instead of just move forward.

# Team Meeting - 9/17

- Added new drive code mode, dual stick drive
- Currently we had 2 drive modes (they are just lying in the code)
- Tank drive (standard tank drive setup, joystick drive controlling each side of the robot, give the most control)
- Single stick drive (one stick controls both moving forward (up/down) and turning (left/right), gives least amount of control yet leaves a full stick for other controls of the robot)
- And now, we added dual stick drive!
- It's the single stick drive but split into 2 separate sticks the left stick controls moving forward (up/down) and the right stick controls turning (left right)
- Although it uses 2 sticks instead of one, it's much easier to not have mistakes unlike the single stick drive

# Team Meeting - 11/18

Inertial sensor rotation problem

- But, when doing turning, since the inertial sensor already had a variable for detecting the velocity of the turn in DPS (so we don't have to do acceleration to velocity) we just used the same system but for degrees instead of rotation.
- But we ran into a problem, the velocity variable (velset, from the code snippet before), just was not being set to floor speed and going up and down and all that stuff (like in original drivetrain w/ the sensor)
- This was fixed with making velset equal (velocity_curve(dist_moved,meters) * (max_speed - floor_speed)) + floor_speed
- Why? Because velocity_curve first returns a 0 and with the one before, everything (including the +floor speed) was times it so it just equaled zero
- Why did it work for moving forward? We think it was accounting for slight shifts on the floor so that gave it a kick start from zero
- We taught the new members how to code some more, and were able to get them to make their first drive code, which used the joysticks to move the robot
